slides.title: Outlines
slides.content:
• Pattern Representation Learning
– Unsupervised Representation Learning (week 7)
– Supervised Representation Learning (week 8)
– Unified Framework: Graph Embedding (week 8)
• Patter Recognition Models
– Clustering and Applications (week 9)
– Gaussian Mixture Model and Boosting (week 10)
– Support Vector Machines (week 11)
• Deep Learning (week 12)
• Revision and Q&A (week 13)
2

slides.title: Pattern Representation Learning
slides.content:
➢ We will discuss:
➢ What is pattern representation learning
➢ Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
➢ Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)
➢ At the end of this lecture, you should be able to:
➢ Know what pattern representation learning is
➢ Understand rationales behind PCA and NMF
➢ Perform PCA and NMF
5

slides.content:
Raw Data
Data Preprocessing
Grayscale Normalization Augmentation
Extract Informative Face Neck Legs
Pattern Representation Horse Long Long Long
Cat Round Short Short
Unsupervised Supervised

Support Vector Machine

6

slides.title: Representation Learning / Extraction
slides.content:
• Representation learning refers to transforming the
raw/preprocessed data into another (possibly lower-dimensional)
space.
• Such that, in the new space, one can perform pattern recognition
more easily.
• Criterion for good representation in different problem settings:
– Unsupervised: minimize information loss (no class information)
– Supervised: maximize discrimination (with class information)
Face Neck Legs
Horse Long Long Long
Cat Round Short Short
7

slides.title: Why Feature Extraction?
slides.content:
• Many traditional pattern recognition techniques may
not be effective for high-dimensional data
– Curse of dimensionality
8

• Patterns may have small intrinsic dimension
Intrinsic
dimensions
(50-100
dimensions)
400x400x3=480000
dimensions
9

• Visualization: projecting high-dimensional data onto
2D or 3D planes
• Data compression: efficient storage and retrieval
• Noise removal: positive effect on testing accuracy
10

slides.title: Applications of Feature Extraction
slides.content:
• Face recognition
• Handwritten digit recognition
• Text mining
• Image retrieval
Text
• Protein classification
Handwritten digit
Image database
Proteins
11

slides.title: Unsupervised Feature Learning
slides.content: Principal Component Analysis

slides.title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
slides.content:
• Probably the most widely-used and
well-known multivariate analysis
method.
• Introduced by Pearson (1901)
• First applied in ecology by Goodall
(1954) under the name “factor analysis”.
Karl Pearson
Pearson, K. (1901). "On Lines and Planesof Closest Fit to Systems of Points in Space"
PhilosophicalMagazine 2 (11): 559–572.
13

slides.title: What is Principal Component Analysis?
slides.content:
• Principal component analysis (PCA)
– Reduce the dimensionality of a collection of observations by
projecting onto a new smaller set of variables, called principal
components (PCs), while preserving as much information as
possible.
y
• What is PC?
– Capture the big (principal) variability in the data
– Ordered by captured variations from largest to smallest (e.g., 1st PC
captures the largest variability)
– PCs are uncorrelated (orthogonal to each other)
14

slides.title: PCA and Classification
slides.content:
• Classification with PCA
– Compute PCs using training dataset
– Project both training and testing data into the obtained PCs space
– For each testing data point, use K-Nearest-Neighbor (KNN) for classification
– Issue: accuracy is sensitive to the number of PCs
Testing data point
How to determine
the number of PCs?
To choose p based on percentage of variation to
retain, we can use the following criterion (smallest p):
𝑝
σ 𝜆
1 𝑖
≥ 𝑇ℎ𝑟𝑒𝑠ℎ𝑜𝑙𝑑 (𝑒. 𝑔. , 0.95)
𝑑
σ 𝜆
1 𝑖
32

slides.title: PCA Remarks
slides.content:
• PCA
– finds orthonormal basis for data
– Sorts dimensions in order of “importance”
– Discard low significance dimensions
• Uses:
– Get compact description
– Ignore noise
– Improve classification (hopefully)
33

slides.title: PCA Limitations
slides.content:
• PCA is not always an optimal feature extraction for classification
– PCA is based on the sample covariance, irrespective of class-membership
– The projection axes chosen by PCA might not provide good discrimination power
34

• PCA cannot capture NON-LINEAR structure
Note: Curvilinear Component Analysis can solve this case. Study this work
if you are interested.
35

• Lack of interpretability
- Each PC projection is a linear combination of all input features
𝑇
𝑦 = 𝐳 𝐱
𝑘 𝑘

e.g., Face Recognition

such as “mouth”, “eyebrows”

slides.title: Unsupervised Feature Learning
slides.content:
Non-negative Matrix Factorization
(non-negative coefficients and basis vectors)
38

slides.title: NMF: Motivation
slides.content:
• Non-negative coefficients and basis vectors
-More intuitive for non-negative datasets (e.g., images, word counts in
documents, etc.)
-Leads to basis vectors that represent parts
Basis images Reconstructed
39

slides.title: NMF: Mathematical Formulation
slides.content:
• Matrix factorization: VWH
All entries in V, W, H≥0
• V: (dn) Input data matrix, where d is the number of input features,
n is the number of samples.
𝐕 = [𝐱 ,𝐱 , … , 𝐱 ]
𝑑×𝑛 1 2 𝑛
• W: (dr) Basis matrix, where r is the number of basis vectors.
𝐖 = [𝐰 , 𝐰 ,… , 𝐰 ]
𝑑×𝑟 1 2 𝑟
• H: (rn) Coefficient matrix, each column of H is called encoding.
𝐡
1
ℎ ⋯ ℎ
